finding: Accuracy in identifying behavioral category (positive vs. negative vs. neutral) decreases with distance from central visual field, but the magnitude of degradation differs by category: negative categories show 3x greater accuracy loss than positive categories across parafoveal distances, suggesting dissociable processing mechanisms.

Active exposure to deceptive strategy patterns via simulation reduces perceived reliability of messages using those strategies, with effect sizes ~0.3-0.4 (Cohen's d), comparable to prior inoculation studies; effect persists across political ideology, education, and age, larger for initially susceptible participants.

Human trait judgments of neutral faces are organized by two orthogonal dimensions: valence (approach-signal features resembling happiness/anger expressions) and dominance (strength-signal features resembling facial maturity/masculinity); both dimensions predict downstream social judgments (threat) via linear combination.

Adults fail to extract simple repetition patterns (e.g., AAB sequences) when items belong to syntactic categories (noun/verb), but succeed when items belong to open-ended semantic categories (animal/clothing), even though underlying pattern is identical; failure occurs despite explicit instruction and category priming.

Higher socioeconomic rank predicts increased unethical behavior across naturalistic and experimental settings; effect is mediated by more favorable attitudes toward greed; priming greed-values in lower-rank individuals eliminates rank-based differences in unethical choices.

Comprehenders integrate semantic plausibility with string-edit distance during sentence interpretation: implausible sentences are reinterpreted toward plausible alternatives when fewer edits separate them (single edit vs. two edits); effect is asymmetric (deletions >insertions) and modulated by perceived noise rate and base-rate of implausible items.
